speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 10 with Israel Kipen. Israel, what message would you like to give future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I can give a message, the message to future generations is that they should make it their business to know their own self and their own history and their own culture. Our tragedy as a nation was to largely be prompted by our reputation among others, which our own people didn't know how to cope with because they didn't have the answers. They were ignorant themselves of their own culture. They were ignorant about their own past. They couldn't stand up to it when they were attacked. They couldn't-- they couldn't produce the arguments when they were questioned. If they-- if they-- if future generations-- and in this respect, I hope that what we are doing in Melbourne is pivotal and the whole world is looking at us, and is amazed at our achievement. Certainly the big American Jewish community can't comprehend how, in the far corner of the world, we have achieved it with our own resources, with our own human resources, and so on. My message is that the-- in the world of tomorrow, precisely because we are living in a world that doesn't keep us at a distance, but receives us with open arms. It was no-- no great achievement to remain a Jew in-- in the-- in the pogroms of Russia, or in the-- or in the-- in the anti Semitism of-- of Romania or Poland or wherever it is, because you were kept at arm's length anyhow. You had to live a life of internal interaction, because that was the only framework that was available to you. Today, it's-- it's a different world. The-- the-- the great leveler-- the TV is the great leveler. We are-- we-- we want it or not, the children are subject to a-- to a universal culture. It's universal. They are-- they are-- they are part of the global village. And in that-- in that environment, it will be a lot harder to maintain a specific identity, especially as-- as the-- the philosophical direction is of globalization of everything, whether it's technical, whether it is social. There is-- there is-- there is not-- the-- the-- the nationalism of 19th century Europe is gone out through the window. The-- the-- the goal 50 years ago still thought that he can maintain the-- the glory of France in-- from-- from the days of Louis XIV and preserve it and-- and-- and keep it at that stage. It doesn't work any more that way. We are-- willy nilly we are in a global setting, and the more universal the culture is, the less-- the less opportunity and strength there is for us to maintain our specific characteristics and-- and identity. Identity is now a very-- a very major issue. England doesn't want to go into Europe because they don't want to lose their identity. They don't want to lose their identity. Apart from the economic issues, they don't want to lose their identity. The pound is more than a-- than a [INAUDIBLE] than a coin. It is part of the-- of the British identity. They don't want to lose it. And so on. They are-- they are British. They're not Europeans. They've got more in common with America, which is also English in origin, then Australia, than they've got with France and Italy and so on and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: So for us, for future generations who are so bold minded, so liberal. Even the most-- the most-- even those who are regimented, they called it street religious, call the behavior, are still subject to the influences that are so encompassing and inviting and attractive and so on. Of course, religion has got one way of-- of holding you. But whether that is enough, or whether that in itself is all that Judaism is.

speaker: interviewee
text: If Judaism will be nothing more but a-- only a set of religious behavioral patterns, and if that is all that Judaism is, I don't know whether that is-- that is a sufficient raison d'etre. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And if there is a state of Israel that is today fortuitously take-- for those people who are not attuned or influenced by religious dogma or religious prescriptions, and have got another symbol with which to identify as a people, irrespective of their beliefs as a people, as a nation, and so on, even that is-- is not 100% assurance. We don't know. Israel of tomorrow is an unknown quantity. We don't know even whether the-- the Jews of the day are separate, and Jews of Israel may not develop two separate identities because they're subject to different influences and different conditions, and live in different-- in different settings and so on. It is-- nothing is certain. But one thing one has to be sure in terms of the future of young Jewish generations is they must know their past, and they must be aware of the contribution of the Jewish culture gave the world at large over its span of its existence of 4,000 years and so on. We have got nothing to be ashamed of. Nothing to be ashamed of. We will stand up to any culture without bravado, but without apologies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Israel, is there anything further you would like to add to your interview?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't expect you to-- to ask me the-- the last question because I don't know to which extent I am entitled to give advice to future generations. I am considering myself a very simple man, and I've got my own views. Sometimes they prevail in the council of my people and sometimes they don't. But there is one overriding consideration, and goes for all ages for all times. Every generation is fearful about the next generation's future. Every parent is fearful about their children's future. Sometimes we don't give the next generation sufficient credit for their intrinsic capacities to direct their own lives. There is more in their natural capacity and potential than we are prepared to give them credit for originally. We are-- we have survived our own way despite the misgiving of our parents, and our children will find their way despite the-- the fluttering of the hearts of-- of this generation's fathers and mothers and so on and so on. But that's-- that provides-- I'm coming back to the same thing-- provided that they know where they're coming from, and provided that they are not feeling that they owe an apology to the world for being Jewish. They don't. I don't want them to be nationalistic. I don't want them to be putting their chest out and say I-- I am-- I am descendant from Moses and so on. That's not the point. All I want the future generations to know is that we have made our contribution to the development of the world in every sphere of human endeavor. In intellect, in-- in the arts, in-- in-- in sciences, in commerce, in-- in law, in-- Whichever you touch, whatever-- whichever aspect of-- of human thought and human-- we are there. And considering our numbers, we have done-- we have played our part. We've done our share. And we are doing our share, and so on, for whatever reasons. What-- call it genetic, call it whatever it is. Unfortunately, our past has forced us-- our circumstances forced us to be always on the outlook and be alert. The alertness of our minds was not a specific gift. It was a result of the conditions in which we lived, because if we were-- we are not alert, we wouldn't have survived. We had to be alert to any shadow of a danger, any shadow of a-- of a-- of a turn of-- of-- of events. And that is how we are thinking to this day. Even if we are not in danger now, we are still-- we have inherited biologically that sense of alertness. And alertness is a-- is a process of the brain which in the long run helps you in-- in everything that you are attempting to do, whether you want to be a doctor or you want to be a-- a [INAUDIBLE] manufacturer, or whatever-- whatever you want to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Israel, on behalf of the Shoah Foundation, I want to thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. It was my pleasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope that I have not let you down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Absolutely not. Israel, would you like to introduce your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that is a very serious matter.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- it is my pleasure to introduce Laura, who is my life companion for 47 years. She's shared with me my life, for better for worse. And has been a part of all the processes of our decision making. We have always consulted in our life about everything. There is nothing hidden in our life. In business, Laura is aware of everything that I am doing. In communal affairs, she was informed and part and privy to much of the behind the scenes of communal life in this community. And she is involved on a daily basis about everything that affects our family and our lives, and as partners, husband and wife. And she-- some, if not a great deal of the credit of what we talked about this evening as far as my life and achievements are concerned in Australia, the credit goes to her for standing behind me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Laura, would you like to say a few words to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if I can be a little facetious, sometimes I haven't just stood there. I've had to push. He wasn't an easy child to get to university. And it's been a very busy life. We've had a-- apart from business-- a very, very full communal life. I don't know of a week that has gone by when they weren't two, three, sometimes four meetings in our home. And while I was never actually on the committees, I was the chief cook and bottle washer. You can call it that way. I was the catering lady. But I always knew what was going on. And I think everybody around about knew that too. But it's been a busy life and a full life. And I think I've got a husband who's done a great deal for this community. A great deal. He's worked very, very hard and very, very long hours. I hope it's appreciated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you both very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Israel, can you tell me about this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a picture taken in August '32. I was 13 years old, and obviously my friends are of the same age because it's a class picture. What is so remarkable about this picture is that the man who's-- the-- the-- the young man that stands behind me went to Palestine with his parents shortly after this picture was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are you in the photograph? Which one is you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is you? On the left or the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sitting to the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And the man behind me is-- is the one who went with his parents to Palestine. And standing next to him, went in, I think in '36 or '37 to Canada, and became a rabbi there. What happened to the other fellow sitting here, I-- strangely enough, I remember all the names of-- of the people, but I can't say what happened to him. But it is my--

speaker: interviewer
text: What are the names of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Other three?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- from-- going from left, the one sitting next to me is Heifitz. Standing above him is Gershuni. And next to him is Bursztyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you come about to have this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- it was taken most probably for the occasion before Bursztyn left for Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how do you have it in your possession?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it-- well, it was because I am in it, yes? I am-- I am the fourth man on-- in this picture. So I got a-- a copy of that-- of that photograph, obviously.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take this photograph with you when you left Bialystok?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, obviously. Otherwise I would have never-- and let me-- I'm just noticing there's another historical point in that picture. It was just the beginning of the zips as they came into use in the-- in clothing. And you will notice three out of the four are wearing garments with a zip instead of a-- of buttons. We all wears sweaters of some sort, and all the three of us have got a zip instead of buttons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Israel, can you tell me about this image?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is the final composite picture of year 12 taken in June 1937 when we have had our metric final exams.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is not an original photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is not an original photo. My original photo was left hanging on the wall in a frame at home. But a colleague of mine who visit us from Israel some-- maybe eight or 10 years ago-- had the foresight to bring me a photocopy of his, who-- because he understood that I will, in all probability, not have the original. So we are very grateful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me where you are in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am on-- on the right of the building just next-- the person to that-- to the building on the right, that's me. And on the left is my sister. This here above the building in the center is my Uncle Kipen's only daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Miriam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go on. Please explain.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there is another cousin a bit further next-- next to that. That's the fellow who brought it to me. I'm sorry. This. Chava Maranc is my cousin on my mother's side. And the-- the other remark I would like to make is about the two figures in the top row in the center, one of whom is the-- the director, was the director of the school. He left-- he left for Israel before the outbreak of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are we talking about the left or right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Left. Starachowicki. With He became a major academic-- educational influence in the early days of the Israel educational system. But the stoical figure in this-- the picture is the person to the right, next to him. His name is Dr. Datner. He was our phys ed teacher, and he was our class teacher taking us to year 12. And he was one of the leaders of the partisans who went through the war and survived in the-- the forests around Bialystok. Took part in the liberation of Bialystok, and lived after-- after the pogroms that started in Poland after the war, you know. The Kielce pogrom, and so on. He moved to Warsaw and lived there until very, very recently, probably well in his 80s, if not older. Living with the memories of-- of the Hebrew Gymnasium and the people. And-- and he-- he was celebrated by the-- by the Shoah Institute in many ways because he was one of the few people who has-- came out alive from the woods of the partisan movement.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me about your other classmate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the classmate and relative. We-- his mother was my father's cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are talking about which person here?

speaker: interviewee
text: The third from the left in the bottom row. His name was Gutman. He, his brother, and his father were rounded up in the first action of the Germans in Bialystok. And they were together with 15 other-- 1,500 people put into the great central synagogue of Bialystok and set alight and perished in the flames of the first action of the Nazis in Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was probably-- probably in the-- in the end of '42, when-- when-- when the-- when the Nazis attacked Russia, when they came the second time to the-- to-- via-- via Bialystok. Because they were there the first time, and they were treated. Yes. The Russians were there. And then when they came the second time, they show their real colors and they started off on the appropriate-- the right foot for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me about this document, Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As you can see, the Red Cross. Says it as you look at it, it is issued by the Lithuanian Red Cross. It is a document known internationally as the Nansen pass. It was created especially by international agreement to give people who lost their citizenship or their pass-- or their documents and-- during wartime to travel-- to travel in the world and not be hindered by the lack of documents. So it served as a substitute for a-- for a foreign passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this given to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was issued to me in 1940. And that was-- that was the document which many of us held onto because that was our actual identity as far as traveling was concerned.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Israel, can you tell me about this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is the official visa that the-- the Dutch consul in Kovno issued to applicants as an end visa to go to the-- to the isle of Curacao, which was a Dutch possession. It was a document that had in legal terms no practical value. But in terms of the escape of a people, it was fundamental because that gave us the-- those who possessed this visa-- the-- the opportunity to go to the Russians and to the Japanese authorities to ask for transit visas to get to Curacao. And in such a way, the-- the escape route was structured right up to the Japan from where matters took a different course, escalated in my previous story when Japan has directed us toward Shanghai. We could not go to Curacao anyhow, because Curacao wouldn't have let us in.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, on application from the-- from the Dutch-- from the Dutch consul, who was, as it is obvious, going out of his way to be helpful, realizing that every visa that he gives, he may be saving a life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why Why is this a negative?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is a negative because as the number of applicants increased and people wanted to make use of it, there came a moment when I tried to help people to obtain these visa, and we did so in a very underhand way by-- by-- by putting over my original name the name of the person who would like-- who would like to have this visa. And in such a way, I have inadvertently became the core consul with-- with the original consul of the Dutch in Kovno, to-- to distribute Curacao visas to all who were interested to have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Israel, could you tell me about this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a document issued in 1940 during our stay in Japan for me to be able to travel from Kobe to Tokyo in Yokohama where I used to go on different junkets, so to speak. We could not move internally without the permit of the police, and that was a police document counter manned by the Jewish Community Council of Kobe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Israel, do you want to talk about this photograph-- this image?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it is-- it is a document that was issued by the Kobe Community Council who acted on my behalf and other Polish refugees on transit in Japan when we have tried to obtain any visas to countries on the other side of the Pacific. That was a temporary document whilst our passports were in the hands of the authorities with the consulates, the respective consulates.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this photograph down the bottom on the left hand corner?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a photograph, I think, that was taken of me in Wilno the year before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me about the people in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my father and mother after they have come back from Russia via Poland into Paris. And that's a picture taken of them walking the streets of Paris. You can see from the picture that they are already dressed in a Western way. That-- that is not the sort of attire that they were appearing in when they were in Kazakhstan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in 1946 or early '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to make a point of saying that part of this photograph has been obscured as-- by the request of the survivor, Israel Kipen. Can you tell me about the people in this photograph, Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a photograph of the four of us, the four brothers, taken at my brother Laz's home here in Caulfield just recently. The man without the tie is my brother Hershl. Henry. He joined the Polish army in Russia and took part in the counteroffensive of the Russian forces against the Germans. Went through all the way right up to the-- to Berlin, took part in storming of Berlin, and came out without a scratch.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on the right of him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is my brother Lazar, Laz, whose home it is. I am standing center next to Hersh and my brother Sim is on the extreme left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me about these two people in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They happen to be relatives of mine. It's my daughter, Aviva, and-- our daughter, Aviva, and our son, Doron. It was taken at Doron's wedding breakfast. As you can see from the flower in his lapel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And can you tell me about this photograph, Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of my mother taken in Melbourne when she was close to 80 years old. She has lived to the ripe age of 84.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Israel, can you tell me about this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think this photograph speaks for itself. You can see that I am in graduation gear. It was taken that the graduation ceremony on my obtaining the BA Honors at the University of Melbourne in 1981.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excellent. Israel, can you tell me the significance of this occasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This-- this was the occasion of naming the Lectureship of Hebrew Language and Literature at University of Melbourne in my name. The occasion was celebrated by the former Governor General, Sir Zelman Cowen, giving an oration about the life and times of Sir Isaac Isaacs was the-- given at the university lecture hall which was followed by a reception for 50 invited guests. At the center of the photograph, you will see Laura. I'm standing on her left. Sir Zelman is on her right. Lady Cowen is next to him. And behind is the Chancellor of the Melbourne University, Sir Edward Woodward.

speaker: interviewer
text: What an honor. Thank you. Israel, could you tell me the significance of this occasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This was the occasion of naming the Judaica Library at Monash University, which we have sponsored in the name of Laura and myself. A plaque on the side of this picture names this Judaica new library in our name. And the people who are in this picture, from the left to right, next to-- I'm at the extreme left. Next to me is Isador Magid. Next to Magid is Henry Korngold. In the center is Professor Pargetter, who was the Deputy Vice Chancellor Academic, who formally opened this library. Next to him stands Laura. Next to her stands Mark [INAUDIBLE]. Then next to Mark [INAUDIBLE] stands Professor [INAUDIBLE]. And the last to the right is Professor [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Israel, would you like to share your thoughts on this book?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I've shared my thoughts in this book, of course. All we see on the picture is the dust cover of the book. The name, 'A Life to Live,' I have taken as part of a Russian folk saying that says that 'A life to live is not a field to cross.' In other words, it's more to go through life than to walk through a field, as it is understood in a simple Russian way. But what it conveys is a picture of the journey, the route that I've taken, starting from Bialystok, and the route that I've taken via Wilno to Minsk, to Moscow, then trans-Siberian. From there to [INAUDIBLE] and to Japan. And then the southward journey from Japan to Shanghai. And the final part of the trip, the arrival in Sydney and Australia. And here we are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once more, on behalf of the Shoah Foundation, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank you for the wonderful help that you gave me in this interview. And thank you, Ann.
